title: In the Seat of Moses
subtitle: Christ warns the crowds: hear the teaching, do not imitate the life

stillness: Be still, and let the noise of the day fall away.
The disputing voices have fallen silent.
Wait quietly for the word that teaches the heart.
gospel: It was the same third day of the week.
The Scribes and Pharisees had been confounded and silenced.
So that the people might not faint without the bread of doctrine,
Jesus spoke to the crowds and to his disciples, saying:
Upon the seat of Moses have sat the Scribes and Pharisees.
All things therefore whatsoever they say to you, observe and do;
but according to their works, do not.
For they say, and do not.
They bind heavy and unbearable burdens,
and lay them on men's shoulders;
but with their own finger they will not move them.
behold: See how the Lord, after he had cast down the priests by his answer,
and shown how incorrigible their condition was,
turns his speech now to the people.
He does not destroy the chair; he honors it.
Upon the seat of Moses they sit — yet unworthily.
See, then, how you yourselves sit upon it:
for the chair does not make the priest, but the priest the chair.
Mark how he weighs their reproach:
he did not say they cannot, but they will not;
not with the full hand, but with a finger and the smallest touch;
not carry, but merely move — neither to come near nor to touch.
consider: Teaching well and living badly is nothing other than to damn oneself by one's own tongue.
Of such, who say and do not, that word of Genesis may be spoken:
The voice indeed is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau.
Most worthy of accusation is he who, holding the authority of teaching, transgresses the Law.
By teaching well and living badly, you instruct God how he ought to condemn you.
They lay heavy weights on those who come to penance, and themselves do not the least.
If you set on a youth's shoulders a load he cannot bear,
he must either throw off the load, or be crushed beneath the weight.
Where the householder is generous, the steward ought not to be a grasping servant.
If God is kind, why is the priest so harsh?
Would you appear holy? Toward your own life be austere; toward others, kind.
Let men hear you commanding small things; let them see you doing great ones.
Whoever desires to be of use to the souls of his neighbors,
let him first have in himself whatever he is to teach others;
for his word will be powerless, unless men first find in him the very things he teaches.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, in your mercy, take from me the failure of good works,
that I may deserve not only to think and to speak good things, but also to do them, and to fulfill them in deeds.
Remove from me austerity and cruelty,
lest by commanding great things and not doing the lesser, I be seen to relieve myself and to burden others.
Shut out from me vainglory and ostentation,
lest I do my works to be seen by men, and seek human glory in them.
Drive far from me the craving for ambition and praise,
that I may not love the first places, nor the greetings, nor the name of master;
so that, humbling myself thus in this age, I may deserve to be exalted by you in the age to come. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Non cathedra facit sacerdotem, sed sacerdos cathedram; non locus sanctificat hominem, sed homo locum.
latinJewel.english: The chair does not make the priest, but the priest the chair; the place does not sanctify the man, but the man the place.
